seo.title: Tak Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Everything about Tak Province: Thi Lo Su Waterfall (Thailand's largest), Mae Sot border town, Umphang Wildlife Sanctuary, Bhumibol Dam, and Karen hill tribe culture. Area: 16,407 km², population: 670,000. Plan your visit.

overview: Tak is one of Thailand's largest and most remote provinces, stretching along the Myanmar border for over 800 km in the western highlands. Its landscape is defined by rugged mountain ranges, deep river valleys, and vast tracts of protected forest. The province is home to Thi Lo Su — Thailand's largest and most powerful waterfall — hidden deep inside the Umphang Wildlife Sanctuary, one of Southeast Asia's most biologically diverse protected areas. Mae Sot, the main border town, pulses with a vibrant cross-cultural energy born of Thai, Burmese, Karen, and Chinese communities living and trading alongside each other. Bhumibol Dam, Thailand's largest hydroelectric dam, creates a vast turquoise reservoir in the north of the province. Tak rewards adventurous travellers willing to venture off the beaten path with wilderness rafting, hill tribe trekking, and extraordinary natural beauty.
geography: Tak covers 16,407 km² across the western highlands of northern Thailand, sharing an extensive border with Myanmar's Kayah and Karen states. The terrain is overwhelmingly mountainous — the Thanon Thong Chai Range and Dawna Range dominate the western skyline — with the Ping River flowing through the narrower eastern valleys. The Umphang Wildlife Sanctuary and adjacent protected areas form part of the Western Forest Complex, the largest contiguous forest block in mainland Southeast Asia. Elevation ranges from river plains around 200 m to highland peaks exceeding 2,000 m near the Myanmar border. The province's remoteness has kept much of its forest and biodiversity intact.
highlights: Thi Lo Su Waterfall — Thailand's largest waterfall by volume and width, a thundering multi-tiered cascade inside the Umphang Wildlife Sanctuary, accessible by 4WD or rafting from Umphang town

Umphang Wildlife Sanctuary — a UNESCO World Heritage tentative-listed wilderness of exceptional biodiversity, offering multi-day rafting and trekking expeditions through Karen villages and primary jungle
Mae Sot — a fascinating border town on the Thai-Myanmar Friendship Bridge with a unique Burmese-Thai-Karen market culture, jade and gem trading, and diverse street food scene
Bhumibol Dam (Khing Kaew Dam) — Thailand's largest dam at 154 m height, impounding a sweeping turquoise reservoir with boat trips, scenic viewpoints, and an on-site museum
Lan Sang National Park — a compact and accessible park near Tak city with a tiered waterfall, forest trails, and a butterfly garden, ideal for a half-day nature break
Karen and Hmong hill tribe villages — authentic highland communities around Umphang and Mae Sot offering homestays, traditional weaving demonstrations, and insight into cross-border cultures
Thai-Myanmar Friendship Bridge — the border crossing at Mae Sot connecting to Myawaddy (Myanmar), visible from the riverbank with long-tail boat traffic on the Moei River below

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov–Feb: The cool dry season is the prime time for Tak. Temperatures in the valleys sit at a comfortable 18–28°C, trekking trails are dry, and Thi Lo Su Waterfall is still flowing strongly from the retreating monsoon. Mae Sot's markets and border activity are at their liveliest.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Mar–May: The hot season brings temperatures up to 38–42°C in the lowlands and Mae Sot plain. The Umphang highlands remain somewhat cooler. Thi Lo Su's water level drops significantly by April–May. Good for visiting Bhumibol Dam reservoir which is at lower levels exposing red-earth shores.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jun–Oct: Heavy monsoon rains make the road to Umphang (Route 1090) challenging and sometimes impassable. However, Thi Lo Su reaches its maximum awe-inspiring volume in August–October. Rafting season in Umphang typically runs October–February after the rains stabilise.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit November to January for the best all-round experience — comfortable temperatures, accessible roads to Umphang, Thi Lo Su still at impressive flow, and ideal trekking conditions. If you specifically want to see Thi Lo Su at maximum power and don't mind rough roads, October is spectacular.

topAttractions.name: Thi Lo Su Waterfall
topAttractions.description: Thailand's largest and most spectacular waterfall, plunging approximately 200 metres in a series of dramatic tiers along the Umphang River inside the Umphang Wildlife Sanctuary. The falls are 400 metres wide at their broadest point during peak flow, creating a thunderous curtain of white water audible from kilometres away. Reaching Thi Lo Su requires a 4WD journey along the challenging 48-km Route 1090 from Umphang town (or a 3–4 day rafting and trekking expedition from Mae Sot). The effort is universally agreed to be worth it. Swimming is possible in the turquoise pools below the main cascade. The sanctuary surrounding the falls is UNESCO tentative-listed and protects elephants, tigers, clouded leopards, and hundreds of bird species.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB adults / 100 THB children (~$6 USD). 4WD charter from Umphang approximately 2,000–3,000 THB return.

sources.sourceName: Tourism Authority of Thailand — Thi Lo Su

topAttractions.name: Mae Sot Border Town & Moei River Market
topAttractions.description: Thailand's most culturally diverse border town, sitting on the Moei River opposite Myanmar's Myawaddy town. Mae Sot has a unique character shaped by Thai, Burmese, Karen, Shan, and Chinese communities, expressed in its multilingual market stalls, gem and jade trading streets, Burmese temples, Chinese shrines, and an extraordinary street food scene offering mohinga, shan noodles, biryani, and Thai-Burmese fusion dishes. The riverside Rim Moei Market sells Burmese lacquerware, longyis, and handicrafts. The town is also a humanitarian hub hosting numerous NGOs supporting displaced communities from Myanmar.

sources.sourceName: Tourism Authority of Thailand — Mae Sot

topAttractions.name: Umphang Wildlife Sanctuary
topAttractions.description: One of the most biodiverse protected areas in Southeast Asia, covering over 4,000 km² of pristine montane forest in the southwestern corner of Tak Province. The sanctuary forms the core of the Western Forest Complex — a UNESCO World Heritage tentative-listed conservation area stretching into Myanmar. Multi-day rafting and trekking expeditions from Umphang town wind through rapids, Karen villages, bamboo forest, and past waterfalls including the famous Thi Lo Su. Wildlife includes Asian elephants, tigers, clouded leopards, tapirs, hornbills, and gibbons. Local Karen guides operate responsible tours from Umphang.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB national park entry fee (~$6 USD). Multi-day tours from Umphang: 2,500–5,000 THB per person depending on duration.

topAttractions.name: Bhumibol Dam (Khing Kaew Dam)
topAttractions.description: Thailand's largest hydroelectric dam, built on the Ping River in northern Tak Province. At 154 metres high and 486 metres across, the concrete arch dam impounds a vast reservoir stretching 260 km² into the highland valleys. Completed in 1964, the dam was named after the late King Bhumibol Adulyadej and remains a major national landmark. Visitors can take boat trips on the serene turquoise reservoir, view the dam from a panoramic viewing platform, and visit the on-site exhibition hall explaining Thailand's hydroelectric history. The surrounding hills offer scenic birdwatching and sunsets over the water.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Free to visit the dam and viewpoint. Boat trips: approximately 300–600 THB per person.

sources.sourceName: Tourism Authority of Thailand — Bhumibol Dam

topAttractions.name: Lan Sang National Park
topAttractions.description: A compact 104 km² national park situated just 16 km west of Tak city, making it the most accessible natural attraction in the province. The park's centrepiece is Lan Sang Waterfall — a charming multi-tiered cascade surrounded by dense forest. Well-maintained walking trails lead through dipterocarp forest with signposted wildlife spotting points. The park also features a butterfly garden with dozens of local species and is a popular day-trip destination for residents of Tak city. Picnic facilities and simple accommodation are available within the park.

sources.sourceName: Tourism Authority of Thailand — Lan Sang NP

topAttractions.name: Thai-Myanmar Friendship Bridge & Rim Moei Market
topAttractions.description: The First Thai–Myanmar Friendship Bridge spans the Moei River connecting Mae Sot with Myanmar's Myawaddy, serving as one of the most active land border crossings on the Thai-Myanmar frontier. The adjacent Rim Moei border market is a fascinating open-air trading post selling Burmese lacquerware, jade jewellery, longyis (traditional sarongs), wood carvings, handicrafts, and textiles at prices far below Bangkok retail. Long-tail boats ferry goods and people along the river. Day-trippers can cross into Myawaddy for a brief Myanmar experience (confirm current border rules before visiting).
topAttractions.entranceFee: Free to visit the market. Border crossing fee applies for entry into Myanmar.

topAttractions.name: Wat Mani Banphot (Tak City)
topAttractions.description: The most important Buddhist temple in Tak city, housing a revered Buddha image and serving as the spiritual heart of the provincial capital. The temple features an elegantly proportioned Lanna-influenced ubosot (ordination hall) decorated with gilded finials and carved wooden doors. The compound is a calm and photogenic retreat from the busy city streets. The adjacent riverside promenade along the Ping River offers pleasant evening walks and views of wooden teak houses — a reminder of Tak's prosperous 19th-century timber trade.

localFood: Khao Chae — chilled cooked rice soaked in cool jasmine-scented water, served with elaborate accompaniments including stuffed shallots, fried fish paste balls, and pickled vegetables; a Mon-influenced dish popular in Mae Sot's Burmese-Thai restaurants during hot season

Mohinga — Myanmar's national dish: a rich rice-noodle soup in catfish broth seasoned with lemongrass, shallots, and banana stem, found at Mae Sot morning markets run by Burmese vendors and widely considered the best in Thailand outside Bangkok's Burmese communities
Kanom Jeen Nam Ngiao — Shan-influenced rice vermicelli in a spiced pork-and-tomato broth with dried blood cubes and pickled mustard greens; a northern Thai staple found at Tak city morning markets
Shan noodles (Sen Mee Shan) — flat rice noodles served with marinated pork, sesame oil, and chilli, a Shan State specialty common in Mae Sot and Tak's highland market towns
Sai Ua (Northern Thai sausage) — coarsely ground pork sausage packed with lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, galangal, and dried chilli, grilled over charcoal at roadside stalls throughout the province
Khanom Bueang Yuan (Vietnamese-style crispy pancakes) — thin crispy rice flour pancakes filled with bean sprouts, shrimp, and herbs, reflecting the Vietnamese community presence in Mae Sot

Jungle honey — raw wildflower honey harvested by Karen communities in the Umphang highlands, sold at Umphang market and Mae Sot weekend bazaar; prized for its complex floral character and medicinal properties
culture: Tak Province's culture is shaped by its role as a crossroads of civilisations and border peoples. The province was historically significant during the Sukhothai and Ayutthaya eras — King Taksin, who reunified Thailand after the fall of Ayutthaya in 1767, was born of Chinese-Thai heritage and associated with the province that bears his name. Today the cultural landscape is even richer: Mae Sot blends Thai Buddhist, Burmese Buddhist, Karen animist-Christian, Hmong, Shan, and Chinese traditions into a multilingual, multi-faith community unique in Thailand. Karen villages around Umphang maintain traditional weaving, bamboo construction, and animist festivals alongside Buddhist practices. The province celebrates major Thai Buddhist holidays, with the annual Tak Bat Dok Mai (flower offering ceremony) during the Visakha Bucha period being particularly vibrant. Bhumibol Dam is also a site of national reverence for the late King's legacy in water management and rural development.
gettingThere: Mae Sot has its own airport (Mae Sot Airport, IATA: MAQ) with regular flights operated by Nok Air from Bangkok Don Mueang, taking approximately 1 hour 20 minutes. This is the most convenient option for visiting Umphang and the western district. Tak city is served by frequent long-distance buses from Bangkok's Mo Chit Northern Bus Terminal (approximately 6–7 hours, 300–450 THB); Win Tour and Nakhon Chai Air operate multiple daily departures. From Chiang Mai, buses reach Tak in about 3.5 hours via Highway 1. There is no train service to Tak or Mae Sot. From Tak city, frequent minivans connect to Mae Sot in approximately 2 hours (120 THB). The road from Mae Sot to Umphang (Route 1090, 164 km) is a scenic but winding mountain road taking 4–5 hours by songthaew or private vehicle.
gettingAround: Tak Province's distances and terrain make private transport strongly advisable. A rental car or motorbike (available in Mae Sot and Tak city, from 200–500 THB/day for motorbike) provides the greatest flexibility for exploring the western districts. Songthaews (shared pickup trucks) run daily from Mae Sot to Umphang on a fixed morning schedule, taking 4–5 hours on the winding mountain road for approximately 150 THB — book seats the evening before at Mae Sot bus station. Within Mae Sot, motorcycle taxis and tuk-tuks cover short trips for 30–60 THB. Access to Thi Lo Su Waterfall from Umphang requires a 4WD vehicle — local operators charter trucks for 2,000–3,000 THB return. Multi-day rafting and trekking packages from Umphang are best arranged through licensed local agencies who provide transport, guides, and equipment.
travelTips: The road from Mae Sot to Umphang (Route 1090) has 1,219 bends — allow a full day and consider taking the morning songthaew rather than driving at night
Book Umphang rafting and trekking tours at least 1–2 days in advance from Mae Sot; demand exceeds supply during peak season (Nov–Feb)
Mae Sot's Burmese morning market (near the clock tower) opens at 5am and is at its most vibrant before 8am — the best place for authentic mohinga and shan noodles

The border crossing at Mae Sot to Myawaddy requires checking current entry rules — the situation can change; consult the Thai immigration office at the bridge for the latest day-trip visa arrangements
Karen village homestays around Umphang offer an extraordinary cultural experience; book through licensed eco-tour operators who ensure community benefit and responsible tourism practices
Thi Lo Su Waterfall is officially closed during peak monsoon (typically July–September) when access roads flood; the sanctuary reopens around October–November
Bring cash — ATMs are available in Mae Sot and Tak city but rare in Umphang and essentially non-existent further into the sanctuary
Respect local Karen customs: ask permission before photographing people, remove shoes when entering village spirit houses, and dress modestly in highland communities
